item.title: 申请CS实习时如何看起来更好 | How Do I Look Better When Applying For Internships in CS
item.description:
索耶拉姆齐

发表于我即将进入大学二年级，我想尽我所能来正确设置自己。 我目前正在 IT 部门实习，并参加一些课外活动，例如编程比赛。 我知道我很幸运能成为现在的我，但我想继续进步，为明年夏天申请更大的实习做准备。 我正在寻找真正让自己与其他申请人区分开来的方法。 我申请了很多地方来获得我目前所在的这个职位，我想确保明年夏天我可以再实习一次，希望这次能在 CS 方面做点什么。 如果你有任何你觉得非常有趣的事情，请告诉我。

乔纳森·卡特雷尔—— 内森·塔梅兹—— 格伦·卡雷曼斯—— 拉斯穆斯·舒尔茨—— Apr 17 '19 2019 年 4 月 17 日



原文:

Sawyer Ramsey

Posted on Jun 16

I am about to go into my sophomore year in college and want to do everything I can to properly set myself up. I currently am working at an internship in IT and take part some extracurricular events like coding competitions. I know that I am very lucky to be where I am but I want to continue improving in order to prepare for applying to bigger internships next summer. I am looking for ways to really set myself apart from other applicants. I applied to a lot of places to get this position that I currently am in and I want to make sure I can have another internship next summer and hopefully this time get one doing something in CS. If you have anything that you would find to be really interesting please let me know.

Jonathan Cutrell - May 27 '19

Nathan Tamez - May 24 '19

Glenn Carremans - Apr 17 '19

Rasmus Schultz - Apr 17 '19
item.author: trojan2021

item.title: 心理健康的重要性 | Where Mental Health Matters
item.description:
让我们直接切入它。 当您走进工作场所的大门，进入工作现场的卡车，或坐在电脑前开始一天的工作时，您不会忘记生活、挣扎和担忧。 与流行的 Apple TV+ 节目 Severence 不同，您大脑中的芯片不会永久地将您的工作生活与个人生活分开。

现实情况是，您的健康很重要——身体和精神。 在美国，我们重视由精英管理推动的个人主义和财务成功。 从广义上讲，西方自由民主国家对社会健康的投入各不相同。 我建议投入更多的时间和资源并更加重视健康，这将改善我们自己和我们所生活的人的生活。

心理健康很重要，原因有很多。 首先，心理健康是一个人整体幸福感和医疗保健的重要组成部分，但往往被忽视。 此外，患有精神疾病的人面临耻辱和歧视，这通常会导致负面的经济影响（2017 年，Bharadwaj 等人）。 其次，研究表明心理和身体健康之间存在很强的相关性。 第三，我们的心理健康会影响我们的工作效率（2017 年，Bubonya 等）。 最后，工作场所条件和环境是精神疾病的“主要可改变风险因素”（2014，LaMontagne）。

我们在做什么来支持心理健康？

我们目前专注于我们认为鼓励心理健康、承认每个人的价值并提供安全保障的三个关键领域。

在工作中建立健康和幸福的文化创造了一个环境，让员工感到受到重视、支持和激励，以在他们认为有意义的工作中发挥最佳表现（2018 年，Goetzel 等人）。

首先，我们制定了鼓励健康行为的 PTO 政策。

我们软件行业的许多同行都采用了“无限制”的 PTO 政策，并通过提供无限的工作时间来吸引潜在的新员工。 坦率地说，大多数无限制的 PTO 政策都是有缺陷的。 首先，无限制的 PTO 政策已被证明可以减少员工作为个人时间的实际小时数。 其次，这些政策消除了雇主在员工离职时为未使用的 PTO 提供补偿的责任。 第三，雇主经常跟踪员工的“无限制” PTO——通过拒绝请求并要求额外的时间来弥补 PTO，使其不再那么无限制。

在 LiveLoveApp 制定 PTO 政策时，我们试图创建一个对雇主和雇员都公平的政策。 我们的 PTO 政策是什么？ 我很高兴你问。 这是员工手册中逐字记录的假期政策。

您的假期时间将根据您在 LiveLoveApp 第一年的开始日期按比例分配。 您的假期将于每年的 12 月 31 日到期。如果您在未使用假期的情况下从 LiveLoveApp 终止或辞职，您将不会获得这些未使用天数的等值货币报酬。

请注意，本政策并未描述我们关于病假、事假、探亲假和丧假的政策。 我们为所有这些情况制定了慷慨的政策，这些政策符合我们对员工的期望，无论他们的职位或薪酬如何。

我还想批评自己，因为我们目前不为未使用的假期支付等值的货币。 鉴于我对上述同行的批评，这听起来可能有点虚伪。 该决定并非基于我们对公司的信托责任。 相反，这一决定是经过深思熟虑的，目的是强烈鼓励员工每年利用所有的假期时间。 将货币价值脱钩会促使我们所有人远离工作。

我还想简单提一下我们的夏季政策。 我不了解你，但我喜欢在夏季的几个月里到户外活动。 我也喜欢在冬天的几个月里出去玩，但我们会再做一次。 这是我们的夏季政策：

我们每年从 7 月 1 日到 8 月 31 日每周工作 4 天。 刚刚被聘用的员工可能必须按照团队负责人的要求完成培训计划才能获得资格。 当假期发生在夏季时，您可以休假或正常的暑假。 任何额外的休息日都将计为休假日。

这是什么意思？ 这相当于每位员工的 PTO 额外 8 天假期，这是强制性的。 虽然我可能想在这些日子里度过野营旅行或远足，但非常欢迎您花一天时间做家务，玩电子游戏或在后院与孩子们玩耍。 无论您选择做什么，都是您的选择。 目标是鼓励我们所有员工的健康行为。

其次，我们制定了承认每个人价值的薪酬政策。

我们强烈申明，无论性别、性别认同和表达、年龄、性取向、神经差异、残疾、外貌、体型、种族、民族或宗教（或缺乏宗教）如何，对职位的直接薪酬应该是公平和透明的.

我们如何做到这一点实际上非常简单。 再次，让我直接参考我们的员工手册。

如果您所在职位的美国工资中位数有所增加，我们将在明年 1 月 1 日加薪。 即使美国的工资中位数下降，我们也不会减薪。 在 LiveLoveApp，同一级别的同一角色的每个人都获得相同的报酬。 当您升职时，即您从一个级别升到另一个级别，您将获得相应的加薪，在您的下一个薪酬周期生效。

这意味着您不必担心加薪的讨价还价，不必担心由于经济不景气而导致您的工资减少，或者想知道您的同事是否因为做同样的工作而比您赚得更多或更少位置。 这也意味着我们彻底消除了工资歧视。

第三，我们已经建立（并继续成熟）一种促进安全的个人成长文化。

我们的一对一会议不是绩效评估，而是为我们提供了一个讨论的机会：

  • 日常工作内容和负荷，
  • 总体满意度，
  • 与团队、客户、供应商等的关系，以及
  • 消除阻碍个人成长的因素。

季度签到让我们能够审查季度 MBO 并确保您对自己的工作感到满意。 这些季度会议旨在为您和您的经理提供一个机会来衡量您完成 MBO 的情况以及您的整体满意度。

最后，在 LiveLoveApp 提升你的职业生涯永远不需要你改变职业或用你的手艺换取向上的动力。 我们也承认您的职业道路可能包括离开 LiveLoveApp 去寻找另一个机会。 鉴于此，我们确实希望您做出终生承诺，同时在任职期间致力于 LiveLoveApp。 我们希望与您一起成长。

结论

总之，我们相信，提高认识、减少污名化并支持提供鼓励心理健康的环境的工作场所，将改善生活，并最终使我们能够为客户打造更好的产品。

我们承认，我们当前的解决方案和能力与完全支持和鼓励我们的员工、与我们一起工作的人以及我们为之工作的人的福祉的预期结果之间存在巨大差距。 我们寻求以进一步缩小这种差距的方式倾听、学习和行动。

参考文献

  • 经济学快报159（2017）：57-60。
  • 劳动经济学46（2017）：150-165。
  • 职业与环境医学杂志60.4（2018 年）：322。


原文:

Let’s just cut straight to it. When you either walk in the door of your workplace, get in the truck on the job site, or sit in front of the computer to start your workday, you do not leave your life, struggles, and concerns behind. Unlike the popular Apple TV+ show Severence, a chip in your brain does not permanently separate your work life from your personal life.

The reality is that your health matters - physical and mental. Here in the United States, we place value on individualism and financial success fueled by meritocracy. Broadly speaking, western liberal democracies vary in their devotion to the health of the society. I would propose that devoting more time and resources and placing higher regard on health would improve our own lives and the lives of those we live among.

Mental health matters for many reasons. First, mental health is a critical component of a person’s overall well-being and health care that is often overlooked. Further, those who suffer from mental illness are faced with stigma and discrimination that often results in negative economic impact (2017, Bharadwaj, et.al.). Second, studies have shown there are strong correlations between mental and physical health. Third, our mental health impacts the productivity of our work (2017, Bubonya, et.al). Finally, workplace conditions and environments are a “major modifiable risk factor” for mental illness (2014, LaMontagne).

What Are We Doing to Support Mental Health?

Mental health matters at LiveLoveApp. We are currently focused on three key areas that we believe encourage mental health, acknowledges the value of each person, and provide security and safety.

Establishing a culture of health and well-being at work creates an environment where employees feel valued, supported, and stimulated to perform at their best in work they find meaningful (2018, Goetzel, et.al.).

First, we have established PTO policies that encourage healthy behavior.

Many of our peers in the software industry have adopted “unlimited” PTO policies and entice potential new hires by offering an unlimited amount of time away from work. To be blunt, most unlimited PTO policies are flawed. First, unlimited PTO policies have been shown to reduce the actual number of hours an employee takes as personal time. Second, these policies eliminate the employer's responsibility to provide compensation for unused PTO when an employee leaves. Third, employers often track an employee’s “unlimited” PTO - making it not so unlimited by denying requests and demanding additional hours to make up for PTO.

When establishing the PTO policy at LiveLoveApp we sought to create one that is equitable for both the employer and employee. What is our PTO policy? I’m glad you asked. Here is our vacation policy verbatim from the employee handbook.

LiveLoveApp offers 15 days of paid time off (PTO) plus 10 holidays every year: New Year's Day, Martin Luther King Jr. Day, Memorial Day, Juneteenth, Independence Day, Labor Day, Veteran's Day, Thanksgiving Day, Day After Thanksgiving Day, and Christmas Day. Your vacation time is prorated based on your start date during your first year at LiveLoveApp. Your vacation time expires every year on December 31. If you are terminated or resign from LiveLoveApp with unused vacation days, you will not be paid the monetary equivalent for those unused days.

Please note that this policy does not describe our policy on sick time, personal leave, family leave, and bereavement leave. We have generous policies for all of these situations that align with our desires for employees, no matter their position or compensation.

I also want to be critical of ourselves in that we currently do not pay the monetary equivalent for unused vacation days. That might sound a bit hypocritical given my critique of peers above. This decision is not based on our fiduciary responsibility to the company. Rather, this decision is deliberate in an effort to strongly encourage employees to use all of their vacation time every year. Decoupling the monetary value promotes all of us to get away from work.

I also want to briefly mention our summer policy. I don’t know about you, but I love getting into the outdoors during the summer months. I also like getting out in the powder during the winter months, but we’ll leave that for another time. Here is our summer policy:

We work 4-day work weeks from July 1 through August 31 every year. An employee who has just been hired may have to complete a training program per their team lead to be eligible. When a holiday occurs during summer hours, you can take the holiday off or your normal summer day off. Any additional days off would count as vacation days.

What does this mean? This equates to an additional 8 vacation days for PTO per employee which is mandatory. While I may want to spend one of these days out on a camping trip or hiking, you are more than welcome to spend the day doing chores, beating a video game, or playing with the kids in the backyard. Whatever you choose to do is your choice. The goal is to encourage healthy behaviors for all of our employees.

Second, we have established compensation policies that acknowledge the value of each person.

We strongly affirm that direct compensation for a position should be equitable and transparently communicated regardless of gender, gender identity and expression, age, sexual orientation, neurodivergence, disability, physical appearance, body size, race, ethnicity, or religion (or lack thereof).

How we do this is actually quite simple. Again, let me reference our employee handbook directly.

LiveLoveApp pays at median United States salary levels for your position regardless of your geographic location as determined by glassdoor.com. The glassdoor salary data is reviewed once per year at the end of November. If the US median salary for your position has increased, we’ll increase pay on January 1st of the next year. We never decrease pay even if the US median salary has dropped. Everyone in the same role at the same level is paid the same at LiveLoveApp. When you get a promotion, that is you move from one level to another, you’ll get a corresponding pay raise effective on your next pay cycle.

This means that you don’t have to be worried over bargaining for a raise, be stressed out about your wage being diminished due to economic downturns, or wonder if your colleague is making more or less than you for doing the same work in the same position. This also means that we have eliminated wage discrimination entirely.

Third, we have established (and continue to mature) a culture of personal growth that fosters security.

LiveLoveApp has an established cadence of bi-monthly 1:1 meetings and quarterly check-ins.

Our 1-on-1 meetings are not performance reviews, rather, this provides an opportunity for us to talk about:

  • daily work content and load,
  • overall satisfaction,
  • relationships with the team, clients, vendors, etc., and
  • removing blockers to personal growth.

A quarterly check-in allows us to review quarterly MBOs and ensure your satisfaction with your work. These quarterly meetings are meant to be an opportunity for you and your manager to gauge both how well you are accomplishing your MBOs as well as your overall satisfaction.

Finally, advancing your career at LiveLoveApp never requires you to change careers or to exchange your craft for upward momentum. LiveLoveApp is dedicated to growing alongside your career. We also acknowledge that your career path may include leaving LiveLoveApp to pursue another opportunity. Given that, we do expect you to make a lifetime commitment while also being committed to LiveLoveApp during your tenure. We want to grow with and alongside you.

Conclusion

In conclusion, we believe that raising awareness, reducing stigma, and supporting a workplace that provides an environment that encourages mental health, will improve lives, and ultimately enables us to build better products for our customers.

We acknowledge the large gap that exists between our current solutions and capabilities, and the desired outcomes to wholly support and encourage the well-being of our employees, those we work with, and those we work for. We seek to listen, learn, and act in a manner that further reduces this gap.

Works Cited

  • LaMontagne, Anthony D., et al. "Workplace mental health: developing an integrated intervention approach." BMC psychiatry 14.1 (2014): 1-11.
  • Bharadwaj, Prashant, Mallesh M. Pai, and Agne Suziedelyte. "Mental health stigma." Economics Letters 159 (2017): 57-60.
  • Bubonya, Melisa, Deborah A. Cobb-Clark, and Mark Wooden. "Mental health and productivity at work: Does what you do matter?." Labour economics 46 (2017): 150-165.
  • Goetzel, Ron Z., et al. "Mental health in the workplace: A call to action proceedings from the mental health in the workplace: Public health summit." Journal of occupational and environmental medicine 60.4 (2018): 322.
item.author: blove

item.title: 自动更新 Twilio Webhook URL | Automating Updates to Twilio Webhook URLs
item.description:
科迪·诺曼

发表于这是我最近遇到的一个有趣的挑战，并且一直是
一段时间以来，我第一次感到渴望解决一个问题
因为我想看看我能不能做到。

已经（检查笔记）大约7
 自上次我写任何与 ruby​​ 相关的东西以来已经有好几年了，大概是 5 年
因为我做过任何演讲或演讲。

我想我没有意识到在经历了几年动荡的岁月之后我有多精疲力尽
个人和专业。 感觉我必须解决一个问题真是太好了
问题只是为了好玩，甚至被迫写下来。

话虽这么说，你男孩生锈了，所以请耐心等待。

最近，我一直在做一个使用 Twilio 发送和接收文本消息的辅助项目。 我一直认为 SMS 是一种非常强大的工具，可以添加到任何应用程序的库中，因为它利用了用户每天已经多次使用的东西。 在最近的一份报告中，Twilio 甚至

报告说， 96% 的消息在收到后三分钟内被阅读，90% 在三秒内被阅读！

这篇文章不会详细介绍如何发送短信。 如果您想了解有关如何发送短信的更多信息，请

这些网络钩子是
每当您的 Twilio 电话号码收到入站短信或电话时都会拨打电话。

传入消息示例

如果对 webhook 的第一个请求失败，两个通道都有一个备用 URL。

向本地环境发送 Webhook 请求

如果您曾经使用本地环境从第三方 webhook 或服务开发过功能，您可能通过将本地环境暴露给

互联网与 ngrok 之类的东西。

如果您还没有听说过 ngrok，它是我最喜欢的工具之一，我越来越多地集成到更多的工作流程中。 您可以阅读有关 ngrok 的更多信息

在这里。

在你的 Procfile 中运行 Ngrok

服务，我已将 ngrok 添加为自己的进程，因此它始终可用。

例子Procfile.dev

 # Procfile.dev web: bin/rails server -p $PORT css: yarn build:css --watch js: yarn build --reload worker: bundle exec sidekiq stripe: stripe listen --forward-to $PORT/webhooks/stripe ngrok: ngrok http --log=stdout $PORT

更新 Twilio Webhook URL

随着 ngrok 的运行，现在有一个 URL 可以将您的 webhook 指向。 更新您的 webhook URL 的一种选择是获取您的 ngrok URL，进入

这对我来说很好……有一段时间。

此设置的主要问题是，每当我重新启动服务时，ngrok URL 都会更改并且需要再次更新。 这可能会在启动和停止服务时产生大量更新，而在忘记更新服务时会出现错误

每次的网址。

另外，如果您不经常这样做，那很容易
忘记如何找到更新 webhook URL 的部分。

那时我有一种冲动，想在需要时找到一种自动化的方法。

在 Twilio 文档中进行了一些挖掘之后，我找到了一些选项来设置
通过他们的ruby​​ gem获取 webhooks URL

多一点挖掘帮助我找到了 ngrok 的端点，您可以查询以获得
这是公共网址。

现在我们用煤气做饭。 用我们本地 ngrok 实例的 URL 和方式
要以编程方式更新 webhook URL，我们有两个我们需要的部分。

下一步是决定当我们需要更新时如何执行该代码。 对我来说，这是一项艰巨的任务。 这给了我一个很好的可重复的方式来更新

需要时提供 URL。

用于更新 URL 的 Rake 任务

在我开始详细介绍之前，这里是 rake 任务的代码。 这
如果您想在终端中添加一些颜色以使事情更容易被发现，请使用colorize gem

这是 rake 任务的示例

namespace :development do desc "updates twilio webhook urls to ngrok url" task set_twilio_webhooks: :environment do return unless Rails . env . development? begin response = JSON . parse ( Net :: HTTP . get ( URI ( "http://localhost:4040/api/tunnels" ))) ngrok_url = response . dig ( "tunnels" , 1 , "public_url" ) rescue Errno :: ECONNREFUSED puts "*******NGROK not running*******" . yellow end if ngrok_url puts ngrok_url . green account_sid = Rails . application . credentials . dig ( :twilio )[ :account_sid ] auth_token = Rails . application . credentials . dig ( :twilio )[ :auth_token ] development_incoming_phone_number = Rails . application . credentials . dig ( :twilio )[ :auth_token ] twilio_client = Twilio :: Client . new ( account_sid , auth_token ) twilio_client . api . v2010 . accounts ( account_sid ). incoming_phone_numbers ( developemnt_incoming_phone_number ) . update ( sms_url: " #{ ngrok_url } /webhooks/twilio" , sms_fallback_url: " #{ ngrok_url } /webhooks/twilio_fallback" , voice_url: " #{ ngrok_url } /webhooks/twilio_voice" , voice_fallback_url: " #{ ngrok_url } /webhooks/twilio_voice_fallback" ) puts "Webhook URLs updated!" . green rescue Twilio :: REST :: RestError => e puts e end end end end

迪茨

现在，让我们更深入地了解正在发生的事情。

在 rake 任务中，我要做的第一件事是确保我们在
可能不需要，但我认为这是一个很好的检查，可以防止意外更新

我们的development环境之外的信息。

在确保我们在我们的开发环境中之后，我们需要做的是找到
运行 ngrok 实例。 此端点返回一个 XML 响应，其中包含有关当前隧道的信息。 有 2 条隧道返回，我用

名称command_line (http)这是返回的第二个。

通常，我选择的 ruby​​ HTTP 库是HTTParty ，但我想使用
在确保我们在development环境中之后，Net::HTTP 然后创建一个
向 ngrok 端点请求并解析 XML 响应。

以下是它发回的响应的一些示例。

截断的 Ngrok 响应

<tunnelListResource> <Tunnels> </Tunnels> <Tunnels> </Tunnels> <URI> /api/tunnels </URI> </tunnelListResource>

单个隧道对象

<Tunnels> <Name> command_line </Name> <URI> /api/tunnels/command_line </URI> <PublicURL> https://7be0-72-42-102-194.ngrok.io </PublicURL> <Proto> https </Proto> <Config> <Addr> http://localhost:80 </Addr> <Inspect> true </Inspect> </Config> <Metrics> <Conns> </Conns> <HTTP> <Count> 0 </Count> <Rate1> 0 </Rate1> <Rate5> 0 </Rate5> <Rate15> 0 </Rate15> <P50> 0 </P50> <P90> 0 </P90> <P95> 0 </P95> <P99> 0 </P99> </HTTP> </Metrics> </Tunnels>

为什么你
问？

老实说，这很可能是 StackOverflow 的第一件事，我搬家了
上。

两个隧道的<PublicURL>似乎总是相同的，所以它是
可能的任何一个都行。

如果 ngrok 没有运行，我们会得到一个Errno::ECONNREFUSED错误，因此我们可以挽救该错误，并以黄色打印一些输出，以便于知道何时

出问题了

获取 Ngrok 公共网址

begin response = JSON . parse ( Net :: HTTP . get ( URI ( "http://localhost:4040/api/tunnels" ))) ngrok_url = response . dig ( "tunnels" , 1 , "public_url" ) rescue Errno :: ECONNREFUSED puts "*******NGROK not running*******" . yellow end

现在，我们有了一种可重复且可靠的方式来在 ngrok URL 运行时获取它。 我们还获得了一些彩色输出，为我们提供快速的视觉指示

一切看起来都很好。

下一部分是使用我们的以编程方式更新 Twilio webhook 端点
花了很多时间才找到 twilio-ruby 中所需的端点
宝石，但一旦我找到它们，就从那里一帆风顺。

使用 Twilio API 进行更新

更新 Twilio 的 webhook URL 需要做一些事情。 这
我们需要的第一件事是拥有我们的钥匙的客户。

要创建 Twilio 客户端，您需要获取您的account_sid和
您可以通过登录 Twilio 找到这两者。 当您在那里时，请务必找到您的电话号码的服务 ID。 这是必需的

针对更新的正确电话号码。

我在 rake 任务中包含了 Twilio 客户端设置，但这主要是为了有一个无需额外配置即可按原样工作的示例。 我通常

在初始化程序中配置我的 Twilio 客户端以将重复降至最低。

示例config/initializers/twilio.rb文件

Twilio . configure do | config | config . account_sid = Rails . application . credentials . dig ( :twilio )[ :account_sid ] config . auth_token = Rails . application . credentials . dig ( :twilio )[ :auth_token ] end

创建客户端后，我们可以将ngrok_url与端点结合使用
它指向您的 webhook 处理程序并更新我们的 Twilio webhook URL。

如果一切按计划进行，我们将以绿色输出成功消息。

如果事情没有按计划进行，我们将拯救Twilio::REST::RestError和
输出错误。

现在，每当我们需要更新我们的 Twilio webhook URL（或将来的任何其他 3rd 方服务）时，我们都可以运行：

 $ bin/rails development:set_twilio_webhooks

找到一种在我的 Procfile 中自动运行 ngrok 并以编程方式获取其公共 URL 的方法对我的工作流程来说是一个很好的发现。 这可以扩展到您可能希望在开发中使用的大量其他服务

环境。

让我知道您自动化的任何其他服务！

迪甘塔乔杜里 - Jun 16 6月16日



原文:

Cody Norman

Posted on Jun 16

This was an interesting challenge I ran into recently and has been the
first time in a while that I've felt that itch to solve a problem just
because I wanted to see if I could do it.

It's been (checks notes) about 7
years since the last time I wrote anything related to ruby and probably 5 years
since I've given any talks or presentations.

I don't think I realized how burnt out I was after a few tumultuous years both
personally and professionally. It was great to feel like I had to solve a
problem just for fun and was even compelled to write about it.

That being said, ya boy is rusty, so bear with me.

Recently, I've been doing some work on a side project that uses Twilio for sending and receiving text messages. I've always thought of SMS as a really powerful tool to add to any app's arsenal since it leverages something users already use multiple times per day. In a recent report, Twilio even

reported that 96% of messages are read within three minutes of receipt, and 90% within three seconds!

This post won't go into detail on sending text messages. If you'd like some more information on how to send text messages, the

twilio-ruby gem has some examples.

Twilio phone numbers have webhooks for both Voice and SMS. These webhooks are
called whenever your Twilio phone number gets an inbound text or phone call.

Incoming message example

Both channels have a fallback URL if the first request to the webhook fails.

Sending Webhook Requests To Your Local Environment

If you've ever developed features from a third-party webhook or service with your local environment, you probably did it by exposing your local environment to the

internet with something like ngrok.

If you haven't heard of ngrok, it's one of my favorite tools that I've been integrating into more workflow more and more. You can read more about ngrok

here.

Running Ngrok In Your Procfile

With Rails 7 embracing using foreman and Procfiles for starting and running
services, I've added ngrok as its own process so it's always available.

example Procfile.dev


# Procfile.dev
web: bin/rails server -p $PORT
css: yarn build:css --watch
js: yarn build --reload
worker: bundle exec sidekiq
stripe: stripe listen --forward-to $PORT/webhooks/stripe
ngrok: ngrok http --log=stdout $PORT

Updating Twilio Webhook URLs

With ngrok running, there's now a URL to point your webhooks to. One option for updating your webhook URLs is grabbing your ngrok URL, going into

the Twilio console and update the webhook URLs for voice and sms.

That was fine for me...for a while.

My main issue with this setup is whenever I restart my services, the ngrok URL changes and needs to be updated again. That can make for a lot of updates when starting and stopping your services and errors when forgetting to update the

URLs each time.

Also, if that's something you're not doing very often, it's easy
to forget how to find the section to update the webhook URLs.

That's when I felt the urge to find a way to automate that whenever I needed it.

After some digging in the Twilio documentation, I found some options to set the
webhooks URLs through their ruby gem

A little more digging helped me find an endpoint for ngrok you can query to get
it's public url.

Now we're cooking with gas. With the URL of our local ngrok instance and a way
to programmatically update the webhook URLs, we have the 2 pieces we need.

The next step is to decide how to execute that code when we need updates. For me, that was a rake task. That gives me a nice repeatable way to update the

URLs whenever I need to.

Rake Task For Updating URLs

Before I start going into more detail, here's the code for the rake task. The
dependencies for the rake task are a running ngrok instance, the twilio-ruby gem (I'm using v5.67.1),

and if you'd like a pop of color in your terminal to make things easier to spot, the colorize gem

Here is an example of the rake task

namespace :development do desc "updates twilio webhook urls to ngrok url" task set_twilio_webhooks: :environment do return unless Rails.env.development? begin response = JSON.parse(Net::HTTP.get(URI("http://localhost:4040/api/tunnels"))) ngrok_url = response.dig("tunnels", 1, "public_url") rescue Errno::ECONNREFUSED puts "*******NGROK not running*******".yellow end if ngrok_url puts ngrok_url.green account_sid = Rails.application.credentials.dig(:twilio)[:account_sid] auth_token = Rails.application.credentials.dig(:twilio)[:auth_token] development_incoming_phone_number = Rails.application.credentials.dig(:twilio)[:auth_token] twilio_client = Twilio::Client.new(account_sid, auth_token) twilio_client.api.v2010.accounts(account_sid).incoming_phone_numbers(developemnt_incoming_phone_number) .update(sms_url: "#{ngrok_url}/webhooks/twilio", sms_fallback_url: "#{ngrok_url}/webhooks/twilio_fallback", voice_url: "#{ngrok_url}/webhooks/twilio_voice", voice_fallback_url: "#{ngrok_url}/webhooks/twilio_voice_fallback") puts "Webhook URLs updated!".green rescue Twilio::REST::RestError => e puts e end end end
end

The Deets

Now, let's dig a little deeper into what's going on.

Inside the rake task, the first thing I'm doing is making sure we're in the
development environment and returning from the task if we're not. Probably not required, but I thought it was a nice check to keep from accidentally updating

information outside of our development environment.

After ensuring we're in our dev environment, we need to do is find the URL of the
running ngrok instance. Ngrok has an API endpoint running at port 4040 for the current tunnels. This endpoint returns an XML response with information about the current tunnels. There are 2 tunnels returned, and I grab the one with the

name command_line (http) which is the second one returned.

Usually, my ruby HTTP library of choice is HTTParty but I wanted to set this up using
the ruby Net::HTTP lib to keep from introducing another dependency.

After ensuring we're in the development environment, Net::HTTP then makes a
request to the ngrok endpoint and parses the XML response.

Here are some examples of the response it sends back.

Truncated Ngrok response

<tunnelListResource> <Tunnels> </Tunnels> <Tunnels> </Tunnels> <URI>/api/tunnels</URI>
</tunnelListResource>

Single Tunnel Object

<Tunnels> <Name>command_line</Name> <URI>/api/tunnels/command_line</URI> <PublicURL>https://7be0-72-42-102-194.ngrok.io</PublicURL> <Proto>https</Proto> <Config> <Addr>http://localhost:80</Addr> <Inspect>true</Inspect> </Config> <Metrics> <Conns> </Conns> <HTTP> <Count>0</Count> <Rate1>0</Rate1> <Rate5>0</Rate5> <Rate15>0</Rate15> <P50>0</P50> <P90>0</P90> <P95>0</P95> <P99>0</P99> </HTTP> </Metrics>
</Tunnels>

The rake task takes the parsed XML and grabs the 2nd Tunnel returned. Why you
ask?

Honestly, it was most likely the first thing that worked from StackOverflow and I moved
on.

The <PublicURL> for both tunnels seem to always be the same so it's
possible either would work.

If ngrok is not running, we'll get an Errno::ECONNREFUSED error so we can rescue that error, and print some output in yellow to make it easy to know when

something went wrong

Grab Ngrok Public Url 

 begin response = JSON.parse(Net::HTTP.get(URI("http://localhost:4040/api/tunnels"))) ngrok_url = response.dig("tunnels", 1, "public_url") rescue Errno::ECONNREFUSED puts "*******NGROK not running*******".yellow end

Now we have a repeatable and reliable way to grab our ngrok URL whenever it is running. We also get some colored output to give us a quick visual indicator

that everything looks a-ok.

The next part is to programmatically update the Twilio webhook endpoints with our
public URL for ngrok.

It took quite a bit of digging to find the endpoints needed in the twilio-ruby
gem, but once I found them, it was smooth sailing from there.

Updating with the Twilio API

There are a few things we'll need to update the webhook URLs for Twilio. The
first thing we need is a client with our keys.

To create the Twilio client, you'll need to get your account_sid and
auth_token. You can find both of those by logging into Twilio. While you're there, be sure to find the service id of your phone number. That's required to

target the correct phone number for the updates.

Twilio Updates

 account_sid = Rails.application.credentials.dig(:twilio)[:account_sid] auth_token = Rails.application.credentials.dig(:twilio)[:auth_token] development_incoming_phone_number = Rails.application.credentials.dig(:twilio)[:auth_token] twilio_client = Twilio::Client.new(account_sid, auth_token) twilio_client.api.v2010.accounts(account_sid).incoming_phone_numbers(developemnt_incoming_phone_number) .update(sms_url: "#{ngrok_url}/webhooks/twilio", sms_fallback_url: "#{ngrok_url}/webhooks/twilio_fallback", voice_url: "#{ngrok_url}/webhooks/twilio_voice", voice_fallback_url: "#{ngrok_url}/webhooks/twilio_voice_fallback") puts "Webhook URLs updated!".green
rescue Twilio::REST::RestError => e puts e
end

I included the Twilio client setup in the rake task, but that was mostly to have an example that would work as-is without additional configuration. I typically

configure my Twilio client in an initializer to keep duplication to a minimum.

example config/initializers/twilio.rb file

Twilio.configure do |config| config.account_sid = Rails.application.credentials.dig(:twilio)[:account_sid] config.auth_token = Rails.application.credentials.dig(:twilio)[:auth_token]
end

With the client created, we can use the ngrok_url combined with the endpoint
that points to your webhook handler and update our Twilio webhook URLs.

If everything goes as planned, we'll output a success message in green.

If things don't go as planned, we're rescuing Twilio::REST::RestError and
outputting the error.

Now, whenever we need to update our Twilio webhook URLs (or any other 3rd party service in the future) we can run:


$ bin/rails development:set_twilio_webhooks

Finding a way to automatically run ngrok in my Procfile and have a way to programmatically grab its public URL was a great find for my workflow. This is something that can be expanded to tons of other services you may like to work with in your development

environment.

Let me know any other services you automate!

Diganta Chaudhuri - Jun 16
item.author: cnorm35

item.title: 为什么我喜欢传统的提交 | Why I enjoy conventional commits
item.description:
如果您不了解常规提交，我会请您在此处查看它们，但要点是，它是一种标准的方式来构建您的提交消息，以便一目了然地理解它们。 例如，假设您删除了代码库中的一些日志，而不是编写提交说

我一般写

chore(NameOfBranch): code cleanup, removing logs

这使查看代码的人更容易知道我没有实现某个功能，我只是删除了日志。

同样，对于一个新功能，您可能会收到一条提交消息，上面写着

feat(NameOfBranch): <an explanationon your feature>

我发现这样可以更清晰地跟踪我的工作进度。

但是为什么我喜欢传统的提交呢？ 嗯，它真的很简单，它让我对我所做的事情更加思考。 在我推动我的更改并打开 PR 之前，这对我来说是一个很好的心理检查。 我发现那个暂停思考的时刻真的让我继续前进。



原文:

If you don't know about conventional commits, I would ask you to check them out here, but the gist of it is that, it is a standard way to structure your commit messages such that they are easier to understand at a glance. For instance, say you removed some logs that you had lying around in your code base, instead of writing a commit saying

I normally write

chore(NameOfBranch): code cleanup, removing logs

This makes it easier for the person reviewing the code to know that I was not implementing a feature, I was just removing the logs.

Similarly for a new feature you could have a commit message that says

feat(NameOfBranch): <an explanationon your feature>

I find that this is more legible to track the progress of my work.

But why do I love conventional commits? Well its simple really, it makes me think just a bit harder about what I have done. It's a good mental check for me before I push my changes and open a PR. And I find that that pause to think moment really keeps me going.
item.author: w3ndo

item.title: Laravel 9 - 单动作控制器 | Laravel 9 - Single Action Controllers
item.description:
桑德罗·朱利亚诺·卡加拉

发表于如果你想创建一个只有一个动作的控制器，你可以使用 __invoke ()方法，甚至创建“invokable”控制器。

路线：

 Route::get('path/{id}', 'MyClass');

工匠：

 php artisan make:controller MyClass--invokable

控制器：

 class MyClass extends Controller { public function __invoke($id) { return view('parent.child', [ 'data' => MyModel::findOrFail($id) ]); } }

纳迪亚·马卡列维奇—— 克里斯托弗·宁曼—— Jun 14 6月14日



原文:

Sandro Jhuliano Cagara

Posted on Jun 16

If you want to create a controller with just one action, you can use __invoke() method and even create "invokable" controller.

Route:

Route::get('path/{id}', 'MyClass');

Artisan:

php artisan make:controller MyClass--invokable

Controller:

class MyClass extends Controller
{
    public function __invoke($id)
    {
        return view('parent.child', [
            'data' => MyModel::findOrFail($id)
        ]);
    }
}

Nadia Makarevich - Jun 15

Shahriar Shakil 👨‍💻 - Jun 11

Christopher Ninman - Jun 14
item.author: sandrocagara

item.title: 房屋强盗 II | House Robber II
item.description:
阿布舍克·乔杜里

发表于你是一名专业的强盗，计划抢劫沿街的房屋。 每个房子都藏有一定数量的钱。 这个地方所有的房子都排成一圈。 这意味着第一所房子是最后一所的邻居。 同时，相邻的房屋都连接了安全系统，如果同一晚上有两间相邻的房屋被闯入，它会自动报警。

给定一个整数数组nums代表每所房子的钱数，返回你今晚可以在不报警的情况下抢劫的最大金额。

示例 1：

输入： nums = [2,3,2]
输出： 3
解释：你不能抢劫房子 1（钱 = 2）然后抢劫房子 3（钱 = 2），因为它们是相邻的房子。

示例 2：

输入： nums = [1,2,3,1]
输出： 4
解释：抢劫房子 1（钱 = 1）然后抢劫房子 3（钱 = 3）。
您可以抢劫的总金额 = 1 + 3 = 4。

示例 3：

输入： nums = [1,2,3]
输出： 3

约束：

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 100
  • 0 <= nums[i] <= 1000

解决方案：

 class Solution: def robRec(self, nums, i, n, beg, isFirstRobbed): if i >= n + beg: return 0 if i == n + beg - 1: if isFirstRobbed: return 0 return nums[i % n] if (i, isFirstRobbed) in self.cache: return self.cache[(i, isFirstRobbed)] maxRob = nums[i % n] for j in range(i + 2, beg + n): maxRob = max(maxRob, nums[i % n] + self.robRec(nums, j, n, beg, isFirstRobbed)) self.cache[(i, isFirstRobbed)] = maxRob return maxRob def rob(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: n = len(nums) if n == 1: return nums[0] self.cache = {} maxRob = 0 for i in range(n): maxRob = max(maxRob, self.robRec(nums, i, n, i, True), self.robRec(nums, i + 1, n, i, False)) return maxRob

Alkesh Ghorpade - Alkesh Ghorpade - Nov 25 '21 21 年 11 月 25 日

Alkesh Ghorpade - Alkesh Ghorpade - Oct 29 '21 21 年 10 月 29 日



原文:

Abhishek Chaudhary

Posted on Jun 16

You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed. All houses at this place are arranged in a circle. That means the first house is the neighbor of the last one. Meanwhile, adjacent houses have a security system connected, and it will automatically contact the police if two adjacent houses were broken into on the same night.

Given an integer array nums representing the amount of money of each house, return the maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police.

Example 1:

Input: nums = [2,3,2]
Output: 3
Explanation: You cannot rob house 1 (money = 2) and then rob house 3 (money = 2), because they are adjacent houses.

Example 2:

Input: nums = [1,2,3,1]
Output: 4
Explanation: Rob house 1 (money = 1) and then rob house 3 (money = 3).
Total amount you can rob = 1 + 3 = 4.

Example 3:

Input: nums = [1,2,3]
Output: 3

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 100
  • 0 <= nums[i] <= 1000

SOLUTION:

class Solution:
    def robRec(self, nums, i, n, beg, isFirstRobbed):
        if i >= n + beg:
            return 0
        if i == n + beg - 1:
            if isFirstRobbed:
                return 0
            return nums[i % n]
        if (i, isFirstRobbed) in self.cache:
            return self.cache[(i, isFirstRobbed)]
        maxRob = nums[i % n]
        for j in range(i + 2, beg + n):
            maxRob = max(maxRob, nums[i % n] + self.robRec(nums, j, n, beg, isFirstRobbed))
        self.cache[(i, isFirstRobbed)] = maxRob
        return maxRob

    def rob(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        if n == 1:
            return nums[0]
        self.cache = {}
        maxRob = 0
        for i in range(n):
            maxRob = max(maxRob, self.robRec(nums, i, n, i, True), self.robRec(nums, i + 1, n, i, False))
        return maxRob

Alkesh Ghorpade - Nov 25 '21

Alkesh Ghorpade - Oct 29 '21
